data.title: GS Enhanced Income, Short Duration Bond And Short Duration Government Funds Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

Q3 2025 proved to be a generally favorable quarter for global financial markets, with bonds experiencing a rally. This positive momentum was largely underpinned by the continued resilience of global growth, as the anticipated negative impact of tariffs was less severe than initially feared at the close of Q2. A crucial development was the emergence of growing signs of weakness in the US labor market, which ultimately prompted the Federal Reserve (USFED) to implement a rate cut in September. A significant catalyst was the July US jobs report, released in early August, which was considerably weaker than expected and included substantial downward revisions (-258k) for May and June, the largest monthly downward revisions since May 2020.

Enhanced Income Fund

Attribution: The Fund outperformed the benchmark over the third quarter of 2025 on a gross and net basis.

Duration: The portfolio's duration strategy detracted from returns over the quarter, largely driven by our overweight exposure to the 2-year node of the USD curve, particularly in July, where it sold off by 24bps. July's sell-off was driven by persistent inflation, new tariffs, and robust economic data, alongside the Fed's hawkish stance. This reversed in August, as a softer US jobs report spurred a rally in 1- and 2-year nodes, increasing September rate cut expectations. The Federal Reserve delivered a 25bps rate cut in September, citing employment risks. However, persistent inflation and an upward revision to Q2 GDP (to 3.8%) tempered market expectations for further cuts, recalibrating future policy. The Fed delivered its first rate cut of 2025 in September, a 25bp reduction that lowered the target range for the federal funds rate to 4.00-4.25%. The Fed also indicated further rate cuts, with the median dot plot, or Summary of Economic Projections, projecting an additional 50bps in cuts by the end of 2025.

The 3rd quarter proved to be a largely positive period for US 1-3 year Investment Grade (IG) Corporate bonds, with spreads tightening by a 5 bps over the quarter, reflecting a generally favorable market environment. August experienced a modest widening against Treasuries, reflecting broader IG credit underperformance, though spreads remained historically tight. The market rebounded strongly in September, underpinned by solid corporate fundamentals, resilient economic data, and robust issuance.

Economic Outlook: The economic outlook for the next 6-9 months consists of a complex interplay of inflationary pressures, a gradually loosening labor market, a cautious Federal Reserve, and resilient, albeit moderating, economic growth. The Fed is leaning on a risk management approach. The downside risks to the labor market have risen due to slowing employment growth, and the Fed recently cut rates to manage this, and communicated a likely plan to deliver two additional cuts this year. Our base case is in line with the 2 additional cuts in October/December. However, if inflation proves stickier and the labor market holds up better than expected, the Fed could cut less.

data.title: Micron: Valuation Of This AI Winner Defies Logic
data.description:
The idea to load up Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) stock in late September paid off well, as the stock gained 75% since then. Besides Micron's stellar FQ1 2026 earnings released on December 17, there also were several other bullish catalysts. Furthermore, the fierce AI data center buildout race between tech giants supports strong demand for Micron's memory chips, which helps the company to exert robust pricing power.

Despite the big rally of the last three months, Micron is still very attractively valued with its forward P/E ratio that makes it look as if it is not an AI-exposed growth company. Considering all these factors altogether: stellar performance, accelerating product innovation flywheel, AI tailwinds, massive share price momentum, and a dirt cheap valuation, I think that Micron is an ultimate pick for 2026. All in all, I am confidently reiterating Strong Buy for MU.

Recent developments

Micron released its latest quarterly [FQ1 2026] earnings on December 17, delivering a robust dual beat against consensus estimates. Both revenue and EPS were significantly higher than forecasted levels, which significantly boosted investors' confidence. Revenue growth continued accelerating with a 56.7% YoY growth compared to 46% growth in the previous quarter. Micron's adjusted EPS grew by multiple times on a YoY basis, from $1.79 to $4.78.

I want to emphasize the quality of EPS growth. First of all, it significantly outpaced revenue growth even on a QoQ basis. While revenue grew by 20% sequentially, Micron's bottom line spiked by almost 58% on a QoQ basis. This was 100% high-quality EPS growth because there was only a subtle change in the number of outstanding shares. The company's operating margin has already recovered to previous record levels and is on track to return to all-time high levels recorded before the first USA-China trade war started.

Thanks to accelerated revenue growth and rapidly expanding operating margin, Micron's cash generating capacity is also becoming much stronger. The latest quarter saw more than a double-fold YoY increase in cash from operations [CFO], from $3.2 billion to $8.4 billion. Expanding CFO generating capacity is vital in a highly demanding chip industry, which requires investments in R&D to foster innovation cycles and investments in M&A because relying only on organic growth might limit long-term potential.

Micron's rapidly growing CFO contributes to the financial power accumulation, which I see from the large $10 billion cash pile as of the latest reportable date. Total debt is just $12.5 billion against Micron's $311 billion market cap, which is almost invisible in relative terms. The company significantly decreased its net debt position over the last few quarters, from several billions to hundreds of millions.

Fortifying financial position is vital for Micron's aggressively innovative business model. The company's R&D costs increased again in FQ1 2026, by impressive 32% YoY and 12% QoQ. Micron is known for its ability to convert R&D investments into new successful products. During the quarter, the company rolled out a myriad of new products across various end markets: AI PCs, gaming, AI data centers, and automotive. The fact that new products are not only concentrated around data centers but are also focused on other high-growth areas is a huge green flag indicating that the management has a long-term mindset and cares about the growth sustainability and resilience of the company's business mix.

I am highly convinced that these new products will be successful, as Micron's high bandwidth memory, or HBM, products are on fire at the moment with the management citing calendar 2026 sold out already by the end of calendar 2025. Demand exceeding supply is not only good in terms of volume sales visibility but also helps any company to exert strong pricing power, especially when we speak about sophisticated products like memory chips. That is why I think Micron has great chances to set new profitability records in FY2026.

With strong volume growth momentum and robust pricing power due to soaring demand, it is no wonder that the management's FQ2 2026 guidance was miles above consensus forecasts. As all the largest AI infrastructure investors during the calendar Q3 2025 earnings season shared their plans to boost AI CAPEX in calendar 2026 further, I think that we are highly likely to see new positive surprises from Micron over the next several quarters.

Finally, there are other reasons to continue buying Micron apart from financials, new products, and AI CAPEX tailwinds boosting guidance. Historically, MU performs well in January with a solid 70%-win rate over the last decade. Moreover, there is massive share price momentum that attracts more and more long interest from investors. A combination of all these positive fundamental and non-fundamental factors looks very strong to justify the reiteration of the bullish case.

Valuation update

Moreover, Micron is still very attractively valued if we look at the forward P/E ratio trajectory compared to other semiconductor companies of comparable size capitalizing on AI tailwinds. Companies with their market caps approximately in the same ballpark as Micron are Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Intel (INTC), and Qualcomm (QCOM). I am not comparing MU against Samsung (SSNLF) despite it is the closest rival in memory chips for two big reasons. First, Samsung is not a pure semiconductor play as it is a highly diversified conglomerate and its memory chip business is just a relatively small part of consolidated operations. Second, U.S.-based companies are usually traded with notable premium compared to their international rivals, which also makes direct comparison less relevant.

I think that comparing Micron to the aforementioned American semiconductor stars is fair because Micron is also a highly innovative company and also demonstrates historically strong profitability. There is a comparison of forward adjusted P/E ratios of the four companies selected for this valuation analysis. We can see that Micron is the only company among the group with its forward P/E ratios measured in single digits while others are far higher. Even with the closest peer in terms of the forward P/E ratio, QCOM, there is a 2x gap.

Risks update

The risks that might concern long investors are continued notable insider sells over the last few months. I am not concerned much about insider selling because executives are all people and they also have bills to pay and presents to buy for Christmas. But insider selling might create cautious sentiment around the stock as the public might consider it as a sign that insiders see no further upside. Since sentiment matters a lot over the short term, I think that it is a notable risk for the share price in the near future.

Business-wise, I want to speak about competition as the most significant risk. As I already mentioned, Samsung Electronics is the primary rival, and this company is much larger than Micron in terms of scale. Samsung's memory chip sales are also on fire, and this company has vast resources to invest in innovation. While reviewing Micron's latest quarterly earnings, I highlighted that the company's quarterly CFO is about $8 billion. Samsung's CFO is twice as high, which underscores the massive scale of Micron's main rival. Besides Samsung, there are other robust players from Asia like Toshiba (TOSBF) and SK Hynix (HXSC.F). While the industry experiences soaring demand from data centers, all players are able to exert pricing power as demand exceeds supply. However, when data center buildout peak levels pass, their pricing power will inevitably deteriorate as well. With intense competition, during weak phases of the global semiconductor cycle, Micron's margins will be under pressure.

Bottom line

To conclude, MU remains an easy Strong Buy for me. The company's valuation looks ridiculously cheap when we compare its forward P/E ratio to other semiconductor stars of comparable size that also capitalize on AI tailwinds. Financial performance is stellar, the new products flywheel keep spinning and getting wider, while strong AI tailwinds are still there.

data.title: MercadoLibre: Even If Growth Is Cooling Down, It's My Top Pick Right Now
data.description:
Thesis

I rate MercadoLibre (NASDAQ:MELI) as a Buy, as I expect 24% upside over the next 12 months based on a residual income model. While other companies might offer more upside, MercadoLibre’s stability and steady growth make it a key 'workhorse' for any portfolio. My thesis rests on three pillars: valuation, growth, and the competitive advantages that will likely sustain strong growth over the next decade. Additionally, I acknowledge the competition risk, which is worth keeping an eye on.

First off, while I don't think the company is 'cheap' at the current valuation, the recent drop in prices brought it to a more 'reasonable' valuation range. Second, regarding growth, I believe LATAM is like a teenager, which has its good and bad sides. I mean, it has the 'fits and starts' and the 'ups and downs' of a teenager, but on the flip side, it has a lot of room for growth ahead when compared, for example, to European countries, which are quite the opposite. Third, the integrated ecosystem, which includes the trifecta of E-commerce, fintech, and logistics, is what helps the company to grow at these rates while keeping competitors at bay.

Company Overview

MercadoLibre is the leader in Latin American e-commerce, and also one of the fintech leaders in the region. Its top three main markets are: Brazil (54% of revenues), Mexico (22%), and Argentina (19%). Regarding its segments, there are three different ways to approach it.

First, in the SEC filings, the company classifies its segments by geography, stating that it’s due to the synergy between e-commerce and the fintech segment, which act together as a whole. This is true since, for example, sellers in the marketplace (MercadoLibre) are getting loans from MercadoPago (fintech), and buyers sometimes get loans as well to finance their purchases in several installments.

Second, in their SEC filings, they divide their revenue into "service revenues and financial income" and product revenues. The latter has traditionally been small, but it grew 68% Y/Y, while total revenues grew at 36% Y/Y, and now accounts for 11.7% of total revenues.

Third, the most 'standard' way to approach MercadoLibre is to divide its revenues into two segments: E-commerce and Fintech, as seen in their investor presentations, rather than their SEC filings.

The company grew 39.5% Y/Y in USD, but when adjusting for FX headwinds of LATAM countries, the neutral-FX Y/Y growth rate is 48.9%. While the USD rate is the current reality for the stockholder, the neutral-FX growth rate is more representative of the business itself, without the ups and downs of local currencies.

Additionally, the company is growing across several operating metrics. This is important because sometimes companies grow revenues just by increasing price but not in the user base or items sold; for example, I mentioned that in my article on Knife River Corporation (KNF). That said, I will delve deep, following the E-commerce and Fintech segmentation.

First, the e-commerce segment grew 35.3% in FX-neutral terms, driven by an increase in the number of items sold 39.3% Y/Y. Unique buyers grew by a more modest 26% Y/Y. This is the company's strategy, and it appears it is paying off. This has several advantages; for example, it maximizes the 'Customer lifetime value' (CLV), since acquiring new customers is more expensive than cross-selling to existing ones. Additionally, thanks to their integrated ecosystem, a user of the marketplace may become a user of the fintech segment, which includes payments, credit, and insurance.

Second, the Fintech segment, which recently became the star segment of the company, grew at a 65% Y/Y FX-neutral rate. In Q3 2025, they reported the launch of credit cards in Argentina, and despite the challenging macroeconomic environment of the country, the fintech sector grew 52% Y/Y for that region.

Valuation

Valuation has strongly improved in the last six months, with the given drop in prices. While there are several ratios to consider, EV/EBITDA is one of the ones that makes more sense in terms of 'historical valuation'. I just selected the last three years due to the levels between 2020 and 2022 that skew the scale of the chart. However, while looking at the TTM ratio, it seems cheap; the forward ratio doesn't seem so to that extent.

When looking at peers in the Broadline retail industry, of the Consumer Discretionary Sector, when comparing EV/EBITDA and FWD EBITDA growth, two things stand out. First, there is a strong correlation between these two variables across the peer group; it indicates a correlation of 81%, which implies an R² of 67%. In other words, this indicates that this EBITDA model is a strong fit for this industry. Second, MercadoLibre doesn't appear to be cheap— in fact, it still appears slightly expensive despite the recent pullback in price.

As a side note, Pinduoduo/PDD Holdings (PDD) could be significantly undervalued according to this model, but that's another story for another time.

Before jumping into absolute valuation, let's consider first the deceleration across various line items of the income statement. While revenue growth stood at 44% TTM Y/Y in Q1 2023, it is now growing at 36% Y/Y. Earnings from continuing operations (which aligns with net income in this case) were once surging at 239% TTM Y/Y; however, that growth has now cooled to 44%. This deceleration is worth keeping in mind.

That said, I'm going to perform both absolute and reverse valuation by using a residual income model. My base case scenario assumes that the earnings growth rate will taper off linearly from its current level of 44% to 4.5% by the terminal year. This implies a CAGR of 23.6% over the next 10 years. I believe it's the highly likely case, even though there could be more bullish or bearish cases that support a Hold or Strong Buy thesis.

Additionally, in terms of a reverse valuation, the sensitivity analysis table suggests that if MercadoLibre's Cost of Equity is 10.21%, the required earnings growth rate to justify the valuation is 21% CAGR over the next 10 years. This sensitivity analysis is considering the target price for the next 12 months, rather than the 'current intrinsic value', since I consider it more standard and more representative for companies, as they usually increase the theoretical value in the months that we hold the stock. Just to clarify, the columns (growth) of the sensitivity model have been rounded to reduce visual clutter (that's the reason for the difference between 22% and 24.2% of upside).

The other secondary assumptions are a payout rate at 0%, following the current rate of 2024. This could change over time for good, but I have to be more realistic and conservative, while avoiding making 'arbitrary' and 'ad-hoc assumptions.' Regarding the discount rate, as I usually discuss in my other articles, I'm not a big fan of the CAPM model, but it is useful despite its limitations and inaccuracies. I believe the discount rate should be more reflective of fundamental risks (competition, presence/absence of competitive advantages, debt) and less with market-based indicators (beta, risk-free rate, and ERP). Due to this reason, the sensitivity analysis is intended to avoid bias.

Risks

The key risk I see for MercadoLibre is competition, and it isn't Amazon that concerns me. Obviously, Amazon dominates specific niches of E-commerce, especially for products made in the USA, but it is unlikely it will dethrone MercadoLibre's dominance. The real threat I see is Asian companies that are aggressively competing to get new customers. There are three key names to consider here: Temu, Shein, and Shopee.

In Q3 2025, Temu grew monthly active users (MAUs) at a staggering 143% Y/Y in LATAM, leading MercadoLibre's new CEO to ask for 'tighter rules' from the government. Shein grew its global revenues by 18% Y/Y in 2024, but is focusing heavily on Brazil, which accounts for 54% of MercadoLibre’s revenue. Shopee became the second-largest player in the E-commerce industry in Brazil in February 2025.

This is reported to be happening in Argentina.

Imports rose more than 40% in February 2025, reaching nearly $6 billion — with Chinese imports more than doubling — as Argentines increasingly turned to foreign goods. In the fashion industry specifically, Pro Tejer reported that apparel imports rose 86% between 2024 and 2025 — and, so far this year, of the roughly 20,000 tons of imported textile goods, 73% originated in China.

There are several other risk factors to consider, but I believe they are secondary. The first one is credit risk, due to a higher concentration of credit cards in the portfolio. Second, LATAM economies, such as currency depreciation and macroeconomic contraction (e.g., in Q3 2025, Argentina's marketplace segment suffered). Third, government regulations, which could ultimately weigh on growth and margins. However, I consider competition as the key risk here.

Conclusion

I discussed the three pillars behind my top pick right now. Also, I acknowledged the competition risk from Asian companies, which deserves careful consideration.

First, regarding valuation, I don't believe the company is deeply undervalued, but instead, it's a more nuanced picture. Some models indicate overvaluation, others show a more neutral stance, and others suggest an undervaluation using conservative assumptions. Second, these assumptions are tied to growth and the competitive advantages that sustain it.

Regarding competition from Asian companies, while I believe it's going to impact growth rates and margins of MercadoLibre to some extent, the overall outlook remains very bullish. I’ve accounted for this risk by baking in the USD growth rate instead of the FX-neutral one, which is higher. Also, I assumed a linear decay (tapering) in the growth rate, largely in line with what we're already seeing. I believe this trifecta of E-commerce, fintech, and logistics creates a moat powerful enough to withstand these competition threats, and it can sustain a somewhat lower but still high growth rate in the next decade.

That said, I rate MercadoLibre as a Buy at the current valuation, expecting 24% upside over the next 12 months in my base-case scenario

data.title: URA: Comprehensive Exposure To An Undeniable Trend
data.description:
I’m quite bullish on nuclear. Aside from the fact that President Trump is clearly focusing on the sector from a deregulation perspective, it’s truly the only answer to surging AI energy needs. With electricity demand rising and clean-energy goals bumping up against grid constraints, nuclear is back in the conversation for a simple reason: It works and is the only long-term answer.

Power grids need stability. Decarbonization plans need scale. Nuclear provides the latter two, and that has implications for both uranium markets and funds like the Global X Uranium ETF (URA), which invests in uranium producers and assets they are connected with.

Clean-Energy Goals Are Shifting the Math

Net-zero pledges sound simple, but in reality really aren’t. Solar and wind generation fluctuate. Battery storage can smooth those swings, but building out such a network to meet the nation’s demand remains expensive and resource intensive. Nuclear fills the gap. It is a reliable, consistent source of power that operates regardless of weather conditions and without carbon emissions. It’s the purest form of clean energy available. Governments that had seen nuclear as a halfway house to their renewable future are now treating it the way they might treat any other kind of infrastructure. Reactor life extensions, advanced reactor design support, and regulatory pathways all seem to be pointing in the same direction.

One can not ignore energy security when it comes to the case for nuclear. Recent disruptions to the supply of fossil fuels have been a reminder to policymakers that electricity systems built on imported fuels can turn into geopolitical liabilities. After being fueled, nuclear plants are capable of running for a long time without much exposure to outside disruptions. That resilience is important in a world where demand for power only continues to climb.

Reactor Expansion Is Changing the Shape of Uranium Demand

Reactors are constructed and refueled with operations measured in decades. Once a reactor is licensed, power companies must buy fuel years ahead and keep buying it to the end of the plant’s expected lifetime. Most of this growth is also occurring outside the United States. China is building a fleet of new nuclear reactors faster than any other country. India is on a similar trajectory as it seeks to reconcile development imperatives with carbon constraints. In the Middle East, nuclear power has been adopted in a policy to diversify energy sources and keep oil and gas available for export.

Europe’s stance has also evolved. And in a handful of countries that once scheduled nuclear exits, plans have been postponed, or even reversed. Others are pouring capital into nuclear projects, such as new reactors or cross-border initiatives to help stabilize their grids. Japan’s slow reactivation of reactors offers a slowly growing demand in an increasingly tight market. This is important because demand for uranium is no longer theoretical. It is associated with actual construction schedules, regulatory clearance and fuel supply agreements.

How URA Fits Into the Picture (and Where It Can Go Wrong)

The Global X Uranium ETF has holdings which consist of established producers, junior miners and also has exposure to physical uranium. The mix of holdings matters here, because different parts of the cycle reward different business models. First, producers benefit from having higher prices. Developers get leverage later as capital returns to the sector. And the physical uranium position provides exposure to supply-demand dynamics without operational risk.

Geography also matters when it comes to the fund's diversification. Through a diversified approach, URA can expose shareholders to more than one regional regulatory or political jurisdiction. That diversification is important in a market where supply concentration is still a structural problem.

Still, risks remain. Nuclear policy can change with public opinion, particularly as accidents or cost overruns mount. Sanitation and closings continue to resurface as political flashpoints. Technology breakthroughs in storage or alternative generation, too, could alter the competitive landscape over time.

Overall, URA is a good fund to capture the theme. The mix is strong, the global access is powerful, and the long-term demand is undeniable in a world where data centers are going to be popping up everywhere. Certainly, you can choose individual stocks in the space, but for those not wanting to make a bet on a specific winner, it's as good a fund as any at investing in nuclear.

data.title: 5 Stock Market Warning Signs Heading Into 2026
data.description:
We are heading into 2026 soon and it looks like the stock market is in the midst of the typical Santa Claus Rally. It's been a strong year for the stock market and this is the third year in a row of gains, which leads to the question of whether we will see a fourth year of gains. The S&P 500 Index (SPY) has been a little wobbly lately, but the bulls still appear to be in charge. However, sentiment can change very rapidly and I expect some big swings in sentiment and stock prices in 2026. Just about one month ago the CNN Fear & Greed Index was at 7 which suggests "extreme fear", but now it is at 45 which is a neutral level. That's a huge change in just about four weeks, so a lot can happen in the year ahead.

I feel investors are being too complacent when it comes to a number of potential risks for the economy and the stock market. There's been a major negative shift in the job market data, the unemployment rate just jumped to 4.6%, and consumer sentiment is also at levels that should be cause for concern.

However, bullish investors seem to be focused on recent rate cuts by the Federal Reserve. Plus, the strength of AI capex spending has helped to boost many key tech stocks in 2025, and recently sent the market to record highs, but I believe this strength has helped to mask some serious weakness in key points of the economy. I worry about the concentration risks in this market whereby a handful of tech stocks are responsible for much of the gains this year, and it also concerns me that there seems to be a whole generation of investors who haven't seen a real recession or a long-lasting bear market.

I have been bullish for many years, but I have been much less positive recently because valuations are at historically high levels, and when you combine that with weakness in something that is as important as the job market, I think that means it is time to be more cautious. I watch some of the leading financial shows on TV everyday, and I am surprised that more hasn't been made about the negative shift in the job market — and I have not seen some other very concerning economic data points get much, if any attention when it comes to mainstream financial news shows.

I feel like some of these economic data points might not receive the coverage they deserve until the economy potentially goes into an official recession and/or until the stock market takes a big hit, but at that point, it is too late to take action to protect your portfolio. With this in mind, let's take a look at a number of concerning economic data points and charts below which I believe suggest that investors should be more cautious when it comes to the economy and the stock market and why this could be a time to increase cash levels:

1) The ISM Manufacturing Purchasing Managers' Index Shows Economic Contraction

This index is one of the most important because it is considered to be a leading indicator of what is to come in terms of the economy. This is why it is widely followed and considered to be more relevant data when compared to lagging indicators such as GDP and unemployment reports. Because manufacturers are first in line when it comes to the supply chain, they see shifts in demand before most businesses. When the index is above 50 the manufacturing sector is expanding, and when the index is below 50, it means the manufacturing sector is contracting.

As shown below, with the exception of January and February 2025, this index has been below 50 for every month this year and in November it dropped to the second lowest level of the year with a reading of 48.2. With multiple months showing this index below 50, it seems like this could eventually show up in the GDP data, which has so far remained resilient.

2) Household Debt Is At Record Levels

As shown in the chart below, total household debt rose by $197 billion in Q3 and it now totals $18.59 trillion. Mortgage balances totaled $13.07 trillion. Credit card balances increased by $24 billion and totaled $1.23 trillion. Auto loan balances totaled $1.66 trillion. Home equity lines of credit totaled $422 billion. Student loans went up by $15 billion and totaled $1.65 trillion. Consumers are carrying record levels of debt, as is the U.S. Government and that could increase the chance of a debt crisis. Debt means consumers have been borrowing money which basically pulls forward consumption. Debt means that someone else has a claim on your future earnings or assets and that is not good for future economic growth.

3) Some Consumer Debt Delinquencies Are Surging

It's not just that total household debt is at record levels, it is also that there has been a surge in delinquencies in categories such as student loans and credit cards. Both of these have seen delinquency spikes and auto loans are also seeing increased delinquencies. Auto loan delinquencies are now at levels not seen since the Great Financial Crisis. These are signs that the consumer is increasingly strained financially, and I believe these delinquencies could get much worse if the U.S. economy goes into recession. If credit losses grow for the banks, they could tighten lending standards which has happened historically and that in turn, slows the economy further which often leads to even more credit losses for the banks.

4) A Chart That Concerns Michael Burry

Michael Burry recently made a comment on X about the fact that when household stock market wealth has exceeded real estate wealth as it did in the late 60's and late 90's, there was an ensuing bear market that lasted for years. When stocks hit this level in the past, it is typically indicative of a top in the market, and by contrast, the opposite is typically true when stock ownership is at very low levels when compared to real estate and other household assets.

5) Some Other Points Of Concern To Consider Going Into 2026

Aside from the points of concern raised above, there are some other data points that are worrisome going into 2026. A recent Bank of America survey shows that fund managers now have record low levels of cash in their portfolios, which represents just 3.3% of their portfolios. It's not just record low levels of cash in portfolios now, it is the fact that there are record high levels of investors who are borrowing money to buy stocks as margin debt recently topped $1.2 trillion. This suggests investors are going all in on stocks, and this is something that is typically seen at market tops, not market bottoms.

Where I Could Be Wrong

It's possible that the job market and other signs of economic weakness could stabilize and then even strengthen in the coming months, especially if the economy gets a boost from lower interest rates and some other growth drivers. There are a number of positives that appear to have the potential to be upside catalysts for the economy and the stock market in 2026.

I think one of the most important upside factors could be the larger tax refunds that are expected to happen in early 2026, due to the changes from President Trump's "Big Beautiful Bill". President Trump also recently stated that a $1,776 "Warrior Dividend" would be paid to over one million members of the military, and he has suggested that millions of Americans could get a $2,000 tariff dividend in 2026, and that he will propose this to Congress for approval. President Trump is also focused on deregulation which could boost economic activity as well as corporate profits in 2026. In addition to all this, the AI capex spending could continue to grow and AI could also boost productivity and profits for many companies.

In Summary

There are clearly a lot of new headwinds for the economy especially with the job market deteriorating, and yet the market continues to act resiliently and seems to shrug off multiple concerns. The bulls seem to be focused on an AI capex spending boom and Fed rate cuts. However, rate cuts have not made much of a dent in lowering long-term rates, and in my opinion, the Fed is not lowering rates because something positive is happening (such as inflation is falling); instead it is lowering rates because the economy is clearly faltering with a growing number of layoffs.

Furthermore, AI capex spending could be reduced at some point in 2026, and that would create another potential downside risk for stocks. The stock market is at historically high levels in terms of valuations, and it appears to be priced for perfection. Because of this, I think it makes sense to be a bit more cautious going into 2026 and raise some cash for what could be much better buying opportunities in the future.

data.title: Fortive: Slimmer And More Focused, But Not Clearly Better
data.description:
Fortive Corporation (FTV) has been casting around for the right model for quite a few years now, and as I’ve noted in prior articles, it has at times felt less like management was following a real plan or philosophy and more like whatever was in vogue with the Street at the time – aggressive growth by M&A, an intense focus on recurring revenue and SaaS business models, electrification/AI, and now business model simplification through spinning off Vontier (VNT) and Ralliant (RAL).

I’m not altogether surprised, then, that Fortive hasn’t done much since my last article, with the shares barely up on an adjusted basis and trailing the broader industrial space by close to 20%. Fortive’s odd mix of businesses makes it hard to benchmark against specific companies, but I’ll note that a grab-bag of companies that compete with Fortive businesses – including Keysight (KEYS), Mirion (MIR), MSA Safety (MSA), Steris (STE), and Trimble (TRMB) – have all outperformed Fortive to varying degrees.

Given that Fortive isn’t particularly short-cycle sensitive, I don’t think an improving industrial outlook is all that much of a catalyst for them. Moreover, I need to see more evidence that management can use strategic reinvestment into the business to drive better than 4% revenue growth and mid-20%’s FCF margins, as those metrics aren’t enough to drive a compelling fair value for me.

An Uptick In Guidance And Growth Would Be Welcome

Fortive did manage to beat expectations in the third quarter, but it didn’t leave me all that convinced that there would be strong follow through into the fourth quarter. Revenue was 2% better than expected (with both Intelligent Operating Solutions, or IOS, and Advanced Healthcare Solutions, or AHS, beating), but organic growth was under 2% and not particularly impressive in a quarter when several industrials saw an uptick in organic growth.

The IOS business should pick up some on continued strength in professional instrumentation and gas detection, and I would expect to see Fluke start to improve as well. Facility and Asset Lifecycle is a tougher call with ongoing weakness in non-residential construction (ABI below 50) and declines in non-resi starts, as well as weaker government spending.

I don’t see much reason to expect a sudden surge in the AHS business. While management has talked about cautious spending among healthcare providers, I’m not seeing that in the results from companies like Steris, Getinge (GNGBY), or Mirion, and I don’t really see what would drive a sudden reversal/catchup.

With the Street looking for around 3% revenue growth next year (at the low end of the 3%-5% growth target range provided at the last investor day), it may take guidance for organic growth of 3.5% or better to get the Street a little more enthusiastic about the near-term, as Fortive isn’t particularly well-positioned for a short-cycle recovery given its business mix and end-market market exposures.

I’d also like to see some organic, repeatable upside to margins in the fourth quarter and in FY’26 guidance. Adjusted operating profits did beat expectations by about 12% last quarter, but higher capitalization in the software businesses and adjustments to incentive compensation helped drive about 20% of that beat and aren’t expect to recur in Q4. Still, credit where due – opex was meaningfully lower than expected even without those benefits and that does offer some credible grounds to look for upgraded guidance when Fortive next reports.

The New Fortive Is… Fine - But Where’s The Sizzle?

There’s nothing wrong with a high-quality boring business that churns out solid free cash flow year in and year out. As is, I think Fortive is well-positioned to do that.

The past fixation on recurring revenue may have been overdone, but the reality is that it left Fortive with a business mix that generates solid, high-margin revenue on a relatively consistent basis, and not many industrials could hope to consistently generate 20%-plus free cash flow margins. That, in turn, means ample cash flow that can be reinvested back into the business (R&D and new product development), used on complementary acquisitions, or returned to shareholders through buybacks and dividends.

Even so, the reality is that Fortive has meaningfully derated over the years. Back when M&A-driven “compounders” like Ametek (AME), Danaher (DHR), Nordson (NDSN), Roper (ROP), and Fortive were in vogue, it wasn’t uncommon for them to trade at 20x or more times forward EBITDA. Now Fortive trades at around 17x my ’26 EBITDA estimate and core profitability metrics haven’t really deteriorated meaningfully.

I think part of the issue is that there’s not as much to get excited about with this business. Fluke’s electronic test and measurement tools are critical in maintenance and condition monitoring, and leveraged to ongoing growth in industrial capex, but there isn’t really a solid “above market” growth driver.

The Facility and Asset Lifecycle businesses likewise aren’t really leveraged to growth drivers apart from an eventual turnaround and recovery in non-residential building activity. Moreover, while I could see share growth and maybe increased adoption driving some upside, there’s also a risk that government spending won’t recover to past levels.

Other businesses like gas detection are fine, but relatively small. Turning to healthcare, I’ve written before that I don’t especially love this collection of assets. Landauer is a solid, stable business and I feel the same about Provation, but I have had my concerns about the competitiveness of ASP given a lack of product innovation, and management’s efforts to target faster-growing market segments doesn’t appear to have yielded much.

To reiterate, there’s nothing wrong with a business (or collection of businesses) that can grow fairly reliably around 3%-5% with less cyclicality and well above-average cash flow generation. The problem is how the market prices these stories; right now the market is infatuated with data center leverage, as well (to varying degrees) as themes like automation, electrification, aerospace, and asset intelligence.

Other than some plays on sterilization, inspection, and asset intelligence, Fortive really isn’t well-placed in those megatrends. That’ll likely be a positive when they inevitably cool (as past fads like IIOT, smart cities, and M&A-driven recurring/software revenue did), but in the meantime it could make rerating more challenging.

The Outlook

I don’t have any major issues with management’s investor day guide for 3% to 5% growth through 2027, and I’m modeling around 4% growth over the next four to five years and a little over 4% over the long term, as I expect solid ongoing demand across the company’s major end-markets (government spending the main exception).

I’m a little more hesitant to buy into the 50-100bp/year EBITDA expansion target, particularly if management is serious about wanting to reinvest in R&D to drive improved depth and breadth of new product introductions and product vitality. The subscription nature of the software businesses will certainly help, as should a focus on increased recurring revenue from Fluke and possibly some improvements within the healthcare businesses, but I expecting less EBITDA margin accretion over the next three to five years (around 100-150bp).

I do see Fortive becoming more cash-efficient (software businesses help), and I’m expecting free cash flow margin improvement above and beyond EBITDA margin expansion toward and past the mid-20%’s over the next decade, driving adjusted FCF growth of around 6% to 7%.

Neither discounted cash flow or margin/return-driven EV/EBITDA produce an exciting fair value today. The former suggests a mid-single-digit long-term annualized total potential return, while a 16.25x forward EBITDA multiple (with a 200bp premium embedded to account for quality advantages like the recurring/software revenue contributions) doesn’t get me past the low-$50’s.

The Bottom Line

If Fortive management can guide for stronger organic revenue growth and better margins in 2026, I can see a path to faster rerating. As is, I think this is a business that needs to prove to the Street that it does, in fact, have a core strategy and will stick with it for more than a couple of years. That may sound harsh, but with Fortive underperforming the broader industrial space by a significant margin over the past five years, the onus is on management to reinvigorate sentiment.

data.title: QQQI: 0.02% SEC Yield Vs The 14% Distribution Yield
data.description:
We recently penned a piece on why the covered call funds explosion was just another symptom of the bubble in place. We also described why such funds are likely to be massive losers over the next bear cycle. We did not think we would get a chance to write on a different kind of covered call fund so quickly after that. Today we cover NEOS Nasdaq-100® High Income ETF (QQQI) and go over why this fund is different than the run-of-the-mill buy write funds.

The Strategy

The fund summarizes its strategy with the following.

The Fund seeks to distribute high monthly income generated from investing in the constituents of the Nasdaq-100 Index and implementing a data-driven call option strategy

Actively managed by NEOS, the Fund seeks to take advantage of tax loss harvesting opportunities in addition to utilizing NDX Index options classified as section 1256 contracts, which are subject to lower 60/40 tax rates

The Fund utilizes a call option strategy that may include both sold and purchased NDX index options, which may provide the opportunity for upside capture in rising equity markets

Source: QQQI

The key take home messages are that the fund is actively managed, uses tax advantage of futures contracts and may use call spreads to prevent trailing in parabolic markets. We have nothing against using the futures to sell calls on equity exposure. Futures actually trade longer hours and are generally 99.99% correlated to underlying equities. You do have some time value and interest rate changes which can create brief deviations, but on the whole, this works. We also like the active management idea, as the buy-write funds have just not gotten the message and that blindly selling calls is a recipe for long term problems.

Performance

The fund has done really well against the CBOE Nasdaq-100 BuyWrite Monthly Index. Note the amounts since inception are cumulative (not annualized). Also note that the fund is relatively recent, starting on January 30, 2024.

So right off the bat, we have to appreciate the outperformance relative to the index. How is the fund tracking so much higher? The answer is actually in the same graphic above. It is selling less option premium relative to its benchmark and hence tracking closer to the Nasdaq 100 index. How do you sell less option premium compared to the benchmark? There are a few ways you can do it. One, you can just sell a further out of the money option than what the index dictates.

The Cboe Nasdaq 100 BuyWrite IndexSM (BXN) applies the buywrite strategy to the Nasdaq 100® Index (NDX). The objective of a buwrite strategy is to buffer downside returns by selling a slice of upside returns. The BXN tracks the value of hypothetical portfolio that overlays a short at-the-money call option on the NDX (NDX call). The premium harvested from the NDX call buffers possible losses on an investment in Nasdaq 100 stocks. The BXN Index portfolio is calculated every 15 seconds and is rebalanced monthly after the expiration of the NDX call, typically 11 am ET every third Friday.

Source: CBOE

The fund appears to be doing this. With the Nasdaq 100 at 25,600, the calls written currently are at 26,000 and 26,400.

What calls would you see on the CBOE Nasdaq Buy-Write index? Well, the Nasdaq 100 was around 25,346 at the end of the last monthly option expiration cycle.

So the calls sold would likely be close to 25,400. QQQI is selling calls far above that level and hence making its performance closer to that of the regular Nasdaq 100 index.

There is also another way that you can sell less option premium relative to the index and that is also apparent here. The fund is selling calls on less than 100% of its holdings. So all of that has helped performance as we are in a bull market (in case you didn't know).

Yield

This part is still fairly funny to us as the fund is paying a very large yield and this of course gets everyone out of the woodwork to start chasing. But the math is right there on the front page.

If you want our 2 cents on the actual yield, you can just go with the SEC yield, which funnily enough is actually 2 cents on every $100 dollars. The yield math is easy to figure out. The Nasdaq 100 has basically no underlying yield. That is the product of horrendous valuations on growth stocks. The Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ) yields 0.45%.

QQQI has a much higher expense ratio so that should push the SEC yield negative. But there is some option income there, so maybe that pushes it back into positive territory? Who knows. But that 14% distribution rate is coming from capital appreciation. So if you think that is sustainable, as in you can get it and the NAV can stay stable or increase, you have to think this madness is sustainable.

Good luck with that.

Verdict

We think QQQ likely falls 50%-80% from here. So obviously a fund that rides on that index, won't make our long side cut. The "less" option selling has worked in the bull phase and likely backfires spectacularly in a bear market. Nonetheless, maybe the managers turn out to be good traders and dodge some of the downfall in the market? We wouldn't want to be betting on that outcome at the end of the bubble. We like generating income via covered calls and it is not hard to get 14% to 23% annualized returns on quality companies if you wait for the right price. You also have to use longer dated options to have any chance of this actually working. But we wouldn't have a chance of doing that on an index using shorter dated options, let alone the worst priced index of all time. We rate the fund a Strong Sell.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: Veeva Systems: Veeva AI Comes At A Steep Cost (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

At the start of 2025, I had selected Veeva Systems Inc. (VEEV) to be my software pick for 2025. Up until the first 6 months, Veeva was indeed outperforming the software space, with the stock up 38% during this period of time.

Unfortunately, after staying fairly resilient until the start of November, the stock lost over 25% after its Q3 FY26 earnings, where management pointed to a slowdown in revenue growth and margin contraction in Q4.

Not only that, despite the management reiterating its $6B revenue target by 2030, investors became concerned about the competitive landscape with Salesforce (CRM) after fewer than expected Top 20 biopharma companies selected Vault CRM.

Finally, what is deeply concerning to me is that the path to $6B in revenue comes at a substantial cost of 1000 basis points of margin contraction.

Despite Veeva’s competitive advantage of building deep industry-specific applications, I am forced to downgrade VEEV stock to a Hold at the moment, with a significantly lower price target than before at $236 per share, representing minimal upside from current levels.

On Track For $6B In Revenue By 2030, Veeva AI To Provide Another Tailwind

Veeva reported its Q3 FY26 earnings in November, where revenue slightly declined sequentially, growing 16% YoY, but still came above estimates, while the non-GAAP operating margin expanded to 45%.

In October, when Veeva held their Investor Day, they reiterated their $6B revenue target by 2030. In terms of revenue breakdown by segments, the management broke it down as follows, where they notably expect the revenue contribution from their Clinical segment (that houses Veeva Development Cloud) to grow from 21% to 30%, while the contribution from Commercial (that houses Vault CRM) to shrink from 45% to 35%.

While the management emphasized that Clinical is their largest area of opportunity in life sciences, Veeva continued to make substantial progress in their Commercial Cloud, where they added 23 new Vault CRM customers during the quarter, along with 115 Vault CRM customers that are now live, demonstrating the expertise of their services to support the complexity of global biopharma.

In all of this, the center of attention was on Veeva AI, where management announced that the first Veeva AI agents will be available in December for CRM and commercial content, with plans to roll out R&D, quality, and additional commercial agents in 2026. Here’s a brief timeline of the rollout plan for Veeva AI that management shared during their Investor Day presentation.

Based on management commentary, they have already started working with some of their early adopters, where results are “very promising.” As I wrote in my previous post, the monetization from Veeva AI will be twofold, where the first layer of monetization will be the platform and the second layer of monetization will be from industry-specific agents that customers will get access to when they purchase Veeva AI.

3 Reasons Why Veeva Fell Out Of Favor After Q3

However, Veeva stock significantly dropped after its Q3 earnings for three main reasons, which, I believe, are substantial.

First, when it comes to forward guidance, management guided for Q4 revenue growth to slow down by 400 basis points sequentially to just 12% YoY growth, with non-GAAP operating margin contracting 200 basis points to 43%.

Second, when it comes to Vault CRM migration, management now expects 14 of the top 20 biopharmas to select Vault CRM, slightly fewer than they originally expected to win. When analysts probed further on potential revenue risks from migration hiccups, management responded that CRM is now a smaller part of revenue and no impact is expected this year or next. Having said that, the competitive landscape with Salesforce can’t be ignored, especially after they said that they were “killing Veeva” in the Life Sciences Cloud, which I discussed in my previous post.

Third, while I argued that Veeva’s competitive strength lies in developing deep industry-specific applications, its path to achieving $6B in revenue by FY30 comes at a significant cost of contracted margins. Compared to the 45% non-GAAP operating margins at current levels, management expects that it will drop to 35%+ as they pursue Veeva AI and expand into new markets to reach their $6B goal.

Revisiting My Valuation

Taking the management’s revenue projections of $6B by 2030 and non-GAAP operating margin of 35% into account, we arrive at a non-GAAP operating income of $2.11B by FY31, which will be equivalent to a present value of $1.38B when discounted at 8%.

The stock is currently trading at a non-GAAP forward P/E ratio of 28. Assuming the same P/E ratio for its FY31 earnings, we arrive at a price target of $236 per share, which represents an upside of just 6% from current levels.

The way I see it, either Veeva has to revise their 2030 revenue target much higher or offer a path to achieving their $6B target without sacrificing 1000 basis points of profit margins.

My Final Verdict And Conclusions

In my earlier post, I argued why Veeva is well positioned to compete against Salesforce given its competitive advantage in building deep industry-level applications. I had also expressed my enthusiasm for the opportunity with Veeva AI.

However, Veeva’s Q3 was quite alarming when it said that it expects fewer Top 20 biopharmas to select Vault CRM, while projecting revenue to slow down and margins to contract in the next quarter.

Most importantly, up until now, my valuation on Veeva has always been based on the assumption that the company will reach its $6B revenue target by FY31 with little damage to the bottom line. However, a 1000 basis point margin contraction is too big a price to pay in my opinion.

With the revised margin projection, Veeva’s fair value is much lower than my previous estimate at $236 per share. As a result, I will downgrade the stock to a Hold at the moment, with limited upside potential at current levels.

data.title: VRP: Preferred ETF With Outstanding Risk-Adjusted Return
data.description:
This article updates my review from March in light of current holdings and recent performance.

VRP strategy

Invesco Variable Rate Preferred ETF (VRP) was launched on May 1, 2014 and tracks the ICE Variable Rate Preferred & Hybrid Securities Index. VRP has 330 holdings, a 30-day SEC yield of 5.30%, a trailing 12-month yield of 6.53%, a yield-to-worst of 5.79% and a total expense ratio of 0.50%. Distributions are paid monthly. As described in the prospectus by Invesco, the underlying index…

…is a market capitalization-weighted index designed to track the performance of floating and variable rate investment grade and below investment grade U.S. dollar denominated preferred stock, as well as certain types of hybrid securities

Eligible securities must have at least one day remaining to maturity, at least 18 months to maturity at the time of issuance, and meet other requirements. The portfolio turnover rate was 20% in the most recent fiscal year and 16% in the previous year.

Preferred stocks have priority over common stocks in the payment of dividends and proceeds of liquidation, but are generally junior to debt and do not carry voting rights. They are considered hybrid assets and their price behavior is between stocks and high-yield bonds. Variable or floating rate preferred stocks pay dividends at rates that adjust periodically to reflect market interest rates.

Portfolio

VRP is mostly invested in securities issued by U.S.-domiciled companies (86.5% of asset value), with a focus on financials (62.7%). Utilities and energy weigh 17.2% and 12.6%, while other sectors are below 5%.

Securities with an investment grade rating represent 82% of assets (BBB and better per S&P or equivalent), pointing to moderate credit risk in aggregate.

The interest rate risk is also moderate, with an effective duration of 2.81 years.

The portfolio is quite concentrated, and risks related to some companies are significant. Indeed, the top 10 issuers, listed in the table below, represent 38.9% of asset value, and the heaviest one (Citigroup) is close to 8%. Five major banks are in the top 6.

data.title: BioMarin Pharmaceuticals: Amicus Transforms Business Case Overnight
data.description:
By the end of October, I concluded that BioMarin Pharmaceutical Inc. (BMRN) was showing growing numbers, yet it came with falling expectations. Shares had fallen to the lowest levels in a decade, despite profitable growth and a strong net cash position, even as some growth momentum was slowing down.

This investment case transformed overnight after the company announced a giant deal to acquire Amicus towards year end, set to leverage up the business significantly, with relatively few tangible near term benefits seen.

The positive share price reaction surprises me somewhat, also standing at the basis of my cautious take here.

Some Perspective

Other than a brief momentum run in 2015, which took many biopharmaceutical firms along for the ride, shares of BioMarin peaked around the $100 mark in 2020.

This changed after the company received a Complete Response Letter from the FDA for valoctogene raxoparvovec gene therapy for the treatment of severe hemophilia A, causing shares to crater to $75 overnight.

This share price and resulting valuation were applied to a business that generated $1.70 billion in sales in 2019, on which adjusted earnings of $167 million were reported, with minimal GAAP losses of $24 million reported. As such, BioMarin was posting realistic break-even levels, yet it should be noted that some $700 million was spent on R&D efforts, equal to 40% of sales!

For the past five years, BioMarin has grown and diversified to generate over $2.8 billion in sales, with revenues having grown by about two-thirds in just five years time. Adjusted earnings were reported at $3.52 per share, but this included about a dollar in stock-based compensation expenses, with earnings otherwise seen around $2.50 per share. Moreover, the company guided for 2025 sales to advance to a midpoint of $3.15 billion, seeing adjusted earnings around $4.30 per share.

A net cash position of over a billion dollars would come down a bit following a quarter of a billion deal for Inozyme Pharma, as announced in the spring.

So far, so good, as the business saw growth decelerate to 4% in the third quarter, driven by softer growth in Voxzogo, its enzyme group, as well as the business exploring strategic alternatives for a potential growth darling, Roctavian.

Valuation Perspectives

Following the release of the third-quarter results, the company guided for full-year sales of around $3.175 billion, with adjusted earnings seen around $3.55 per share, down quite a bit from the previous outlook, driven by IPR&D charges related to the Inozyme acquisition. With 192 million shares trading around the $52 mark, the equity valuation of the firm stood at $10 billion, this including a $1.4 billion net cash position. Operating asset valuations of $8.6 billion valued operations at less than 3 times sales, with real earnings support coming in.

However, with operating assets trading around $45 per share, earnings power around $3 and change looked very cheap at a low double-digit earnings multiple. This comes as investors were digesting more competition for top-selling drug Voxzogo, while Rocavian was acknowledged to be a failure now as well.

These created threats to a $4 billion revenue target for 2027, but weighing it all together, valuations have come in amidst continued doubts. Hence, I conclude that speculative appeal was apparent, always speculative if you are dealing with biotechnology names.

A Big Move

Over the past two months, shares of BioMarin have risen substantially from $52 to $60, as some big news has taken place recently. One piece of good news came days after the release of the third quarter results, as the company announced that the FDA accepted BioMarin's PALYNZIQ supplemental biologics license applications for priority review for adolescents aged between 12-17 years.

The bigger news came in the second half of December, when the business announced a huge $4.8 billion deal to acquire Amicus Therapeutics (FOLD), to establish a position in rare diseases. Under the terms of the deal, Amicus will be bought for $14.50 per share, marking a 33% premium over the unaffected closing price. This values equity at $4.8 billion, as the business operated with a net debt load just over a hundred million as well.

With the purchase, BioMarin will obtain products like Galafold, an oral treatment for Fabry disease, and Pombiliti + Opfolda, a two-component therapy for Pompe disease. Moreover, BioMarin will also obtain U.S. rights for DMX-200, a potential small molecule to treat focal segmental glomerulosclerosis.

These products generate $599 million in trailing sales, indicating that the purchase comes at a price of over 8 times sales, compared to just about 3 times for its own business. Net product sales already came in at $169 million in the third quarter, trending close to $675 million each year, while third-quarter sales were up 20% on the year before.

The deal has quite an impact. In between a pre-deal net cash position of around $1.4 billion, I see pro forma net debt of around $3.5 billion, as Amicus is essentially breaking even here. The company has not indicated the current level of leverage but hopes to deleverage to <2.5 times within two years, as the deal is considered accretive to 2027 earnings, with immediate gross leverage seen around 3.0-3.5 times.

Nonetheless, the deal is expensive, as a $4.9 billion deal (including modest net debt) is equivalent to about 57% of the prevailing pre-deal valuation of BioMarin, while the sales contribution of Amicus is seen just shy of 20%!

This huge valuation discrepancy is justified (apparently) by the potential and hopes for management that both products for Galafold and Pompe each can become a >$1 billion drug in the 2030s.

Moving Higher

Despite paying a big premium, higher sales multiples, and jacking up leverage, shares of BioMarin have moved convincingly higher in response to the deal announcement.

Shares rose nearly eight dollars on the announcement, adding about $1.5 billion to the market value of the firm in a single move. That move is big, with shares of acquiring firms typically falling. The move is furthermore substantial in relation to the value of the acquired assets. This comes after the deal ignites some growth, diversification, and also leaves the potential for synergies, with both firms targeting orphan diseases, creating potential synergies across marketing and distribution.

Frankly, I am surprised by the positive reaction, but the reality is that the investment thesis changed dramatically overnight. BioMarin is moving from a substantial net cash position to a substantial net debt load, yet adding some growth engines and diversification to the business as well.

This makes me a bit cautious here, also given the positive reaction. While I understand some state rationale for the moves seen, I am erring on the cautious side, certainly after valuations have increased quite a bit with the move higher, the premium paid, and the debt incurred.
